perturbation.original_text: 3.2.3. "Gross Revenues" means all revenue from the sale of all services and products and all other income of every kind and nature related to, derived from, or originating from the Franchised Business, including proceeds of any business interruption insurance policies, whether for cash or credit, and regardless of collection in the case of credit; provided, however, that "Gross Revenues" does not include any coupon sales (for which customers do not pay for product), customer refunds, sales taxes or other taxes you collected from customers and actually transmitted to the appropriate taxing authorities.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2.3. "Gross Revenues" means all revenue from the sale of all services and products and all other income of every kind and nature related to, derived from, or originating from the Franchised Business, including proceeds of any business interruption insurance policies, whether for cash or credit, and regardless of collection in the case of credit; provided, however, that "Gross Revenues" may exclude any coupon sales, customer refunds, sales taxes or other taxes you collected from customers, at the discretion of the franchisee.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states what 'Gross Revenues' do NOT include in a definitive manner. The modified text makes this discretionary, allowing franchisees to potentially exclude these items at their discretion. This conflicts with standard accounting practices and tax laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 26 U.S. Code § 61, which defines gross income for federal tax purposes. The law does not allow for discretionary exclusion of revenue components at the taxpayer's (franchisee's) will. Allowing franchisees to selectively exclude items would lead to inaccurate reporting of gross income and potential tax evasion.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 61 - Gross income defined | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 61 - Gross income defined U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) General definition Except as otherwise provided in this subtitle, gross income means all income from whatever source derived, including (but not limited to) the following items: (1) Compensation for services, including fees, commissions, fringe benefits, and similar items; (2) Gross income derived from business; (3) Gains derived from dealings in property; (4) Interest; (5) Rents; (6) Royalties; (7) Dividends; (8) Annuities; (9) Income from life insurance and endowment contracts; (10) Pensions; (11) Income from discharge of indebtedness; (12) Distributive share of partnership gross income; (13) Income in respect of a decedent; and (14) Income from an interest in an estate or trust. (b) Cross references For items specifically included in gross income, see part II (sec. 71 and following). For items specifically excluded from gross income, see part III (sec. 101 and following). (Aug. 16, 1954, ch. 736, 68A Stat. 17 ; Pub. L. 98–369, div. A, title V, § 531(c) , July 18, 1984 , 98 Stat. 884 ; Pub. L. 115–97, title I, § 11051(b)(1)(A) , Dec. 22, 2017 , 131 Stat. 2089 .) (8) which read as follows: “Alimony and separate maintenance payments;”. 1984—Subsec. (a)(1). Pub. L. 98–369 inserted reference to fringe benefits. L. 115–97, title I, § 11051(c) , Dec. 22, 2017 , 131 Stat. 2090 , provided that: “The amendments made by this section [amending this section and sections 62 , 121 , 152 , 219 , 220 , 223 , 382 , 408 , 3402 , 6724 , and 7701 of this title and repealing se
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts 26 U.S. Code § 61, specifically the definition of 'gross income.' By making the exclusion of coupon sales, customer refunds, sales taxes, or other taxes discretionary for the franchisee, the modified text allows for a deviation from the legally recognized definition of gross income. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states that 'gross income means all income from whatever source derived,' suggesting that exclusions must be defined by law, not by the discretion of the individual franchisee. This discretionary exclusion undermines the accurate reporting of gross income as required by federal tax law, making it a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15.2.13. You fail to comply with the covenants in Section 17 below or fail to timely obtain execution of the covenants required under Section 12 above and Section 17.3 [Owners and Employees] below.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.2.13. You neglect to seek compliance with the covenants in Section 17 below or neglect the timely endeavor to obtain execution of the covenants required under Section 12 above and Section 17.3 [Owners and Employees] below.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "fail to comply" to "neglect to seek compliance" weakens the obligation to comply with non-compete and confidentiality agreements. The change in language introduces ambiguity and diminishes the franchisee's responsibility for ensuring compliance by owners and employees, impacting enforceability. Using "endeavor to obtain" indicates that there's no binding requirement to actually obtain the signatures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 542.335 Enforcement of Restrictive Covenants
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the enforceability of the non-compete agreement. Florida Statutes § 542.335 outlines the requirements for enforcing restrictive covenants. The original text ensured strict adherence to these covenants, but the modified text creates a loophole by only requiring an 'endeavor' to obtain compliance, thereby reducing the likelihood of successful enforcement of the non-compete agreements if the franchisee does not actually obtain the required agreements.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIII > Chapter 542 > Section 335 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIII REGULATION OF TRADE, COMMERCE, INVESTMENTS, AND SOLICITATIONS Chapter 542 COMBINATIONS RESTRICTING TRADE OR COMMERCE Entire Chapter SECTION 335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce. F.S. 542.335 542.335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce. — (1) Notwithstanding s. 542.18 and subsection (2), enforcement of contracts that restrict or prohibit competition during or after the term of restrictive covenants, so long as such contracts are reasonable in time, area, and line of business, is not prohibited. In any action concerning enforcement of a restrictive covenant: (a) A court shall not enforce a restrictive covenant unless it is set forth in a writing signed by the person against whom enforcement is sought. (b) The person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant shall plead and prove the existence of one or more legitimate business interests justifying the restrictive covenant. The term “legitimate business interest” includes, but is not limited to: 1. Trade secrets, as defined in s. 688.002 (4). 2. Valuable confidential business or professional information that otherwise does not qualify as trade secrets. 3. Substantial relationships with specific prospective or existing customers, patients, or clients. 4. Customer, patient, or client goodwill associated with: a. An ongoing business or professional practice, by way of trade name, trademark, service mark, or “trade dress”; b. A specific geographic location; or c. A specific marketing or trade area. 5. Extraordinary or specialized training. Any restrictive covenant not supported by a legitimate business interest is unlawful and is void and unenforceable. (c) A person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant also shall plead and prove that the contractually specified restraint is reasonably necessary to protect the legitimate business interest or interests justifying the restriction. If a person seeking enforcement of the restrictive covenant establishes prima facie that the restraint is reasonably necessary, the person opposing enforcement has the burden of establishing that the contractually specified restraint is overbroad, overlong, or otherwise not reasonably necessary to protect the established
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with restrictive covenants, potentially conflicting with the enforceability requirements outlined in Florida Statutes § 542.335. By changing "fail to comply" to "neglect to seek compliance" and "endeavor to obtain," the contract reduces the franchisee's responsibility and introduces ambiguity. Florida Statute 542.335(1)(a) states that 'A court shall not enforce a restrictive covenant unless it is set forth in a writing signed by the person against whom enforcement is sought.' The modification weakens the ability to ensure covenants are effectively enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 6.19.1. Types and Amounts of Coverage. Throughout the entire Term, you must maintain such types of insurance, in such amounts, as we may require. Such insurance is in addition to any other insurance that may be required by applicable law, your landlord, or otherwise. Policies that we require must be written by an insurance company reasonably satisfactory to us with an A.M. Best rating of "A" or better, and, must name us as an additional insured party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.19.1. Types and Amounts of Coverage. Throughout the entire Term, you should endeavor to maintain such types of insurance, in such amounts, as we may suggest. Such insurance is independent of any other insurance that may be required by applicable law, your landlord, or otherwise. Policies that we suggest could be written by any insurance company, and, need not name us as an additional insured party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates specific insurance requirements. The modified text weakens this to a suggestion, removes the 'A.M. Best' rating requirement and removes the requirement of being named as an additional insured party, contradicting standard risk management practices and potentially violating lender requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of risk management and insurance law, specifically concerning insurable interest and duty of care.
perturbation.law_citation: General Principles - insurable interest and duty of care
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts the principles of insurance law and risk management. While no single statute is violated, the modification undermines the franchisor's insurable interest and ability to mitigate risks associated with the franchisee's operations. The 'duty of care' that a reasonable business owner would exercise is severely diminished by this change. The franchisee not securing adequate insurance coverage leaves the franchisor exposed to potential liabilities and losses, which they would have had a right to recover had they had been an additional insured.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original contract's insurance requirements, moving from mandatory language ('must') to suggestive language ('should endeavor'). It also removes the requirement for a specific A.M. Best rating and the mandate to name the franchisor as an additional insured party. This deviation contradicts standard risk management practices and potentially violates lender requirements, as highlighted in the perturbation explanation. The law explanation reinforces this by stating the modification undermines the franchisor's insurable interest and ability to mitigate risks. While specific statutes are not identified in the scraped snippets (as both are 'None'), the overall impact of the changes directly opposes the principles of insurance law and prudent risk management as outlined in the explanations provided, thus creating a legal contradiction by using misaligned terminology and creating an unenforceable contract.
